tweets.text: One of our engineers built an interactive math art generator using the new 3.6 Flash model. You can customize speed, colors, and geometry parameters on the fly, then export the design directly to a 3D-printable STL file. Watch the video to see how the physical print turned out! https://t.co/MVivWkv0Nk

Today’s launches are all about better performance, lower latency, and a smaller bill.

+ 3.6 Flash cuts token usage by up to 65% on complex coding
+ 3.5 Flash-Lite reaches speeds of 350 output tokens/sec

Both are live in the Gemini app today!

Next up: Gemini 3.5 Pro, which has officially entered partner testing.

Enterprises, however, can never seem to get enough of it. 

Best combination of price, intelligence, and speed. https://t.co/dkSQWKP60I

The more I’ve relied on my second brain, the dumber my main brain has become.

I’m realizing there’s tremendous value in carrying around a lot of facts, half-baked ideas, and loose threads in your head.

Your subconscious keeps cooking. It makes connections between ideas, extends them, and generates new ones. 

You also have much better real-time recall during conversations, which lets you connect ideas and think faster.

I believe there’s value in a second brain. figuring out how to use one without my main brain getting weaker.

x.name: Aaron Levie
x.bio: ceo @box - your business lives in content. unleash it with AI

tweets.text:
If you were wondering how powerful AI is getting, Agents are now capable of escaping out of systems, finding their way to the internet, discovering zero day security vulnerabilities along the way, and then breaking into external systems - all in an attempt to complete their goal.

Ironically, the ultimate way we’re going to defend against these new risks is equally by throwing compute (in the form of AI) at our code bases, networks, and other systems. You’re going to want vastly more AI on the side of defense as you do on the side of offense. 

We’re entering a new era of what’s going to be possible with AI. Wild times ahead.

tweets.text:
Genius AI has everything 

Building AI tools for small businesses in the real world - hair stylists, massage therapists, other service providers

Nearing $200M in revenue

Founded by a pair of brilliant twins, each of whom does as much as two people

Mandate of Heaven detected https://t.co/YLQiXgbFuw https://t.co/IQN23EPM7D

Bravo. Chris & team have really created a refuge for people who think in public, warding off threats to independent writing from censorship to, now, the slopocalypse 

“The problem is not people using AI, it’s Claudefishing; the deception when you think you’re getting a human connection but there’s no one on the other end.

The problem for the platform is that when “content” made by no one takes over, it drives out human voices and pollutes the commons.

When readers have to wonder if what they’re reading is real, it undermines trust in authorship and threatens the livelihood of writers—including those who use AI tools thoughtfully to produce work they believe in.

Platforms that reward fakeness will drive a race to the bottom. @pangram’s data shows that as much 40% of long-form text on LinkedIn is generated.

We don’t have all the answers, but we don't want to wait to act until it's too late. So as a first step we’re adding the ability to scan to give some transparency and a basis for feedback: what do you want to see?”

x.name: alphaXiv
x.bio: High fidelity research. Dm for promo

tweets.text:
read more:
https://t.co/sdpULPQZzT

This paper shows that pre-training loss can predict post-RL pass@1!

“Understanding Reasoning from Pretraining to Post-Training”

RL for reasoning is usually studied after pretraining, but this paper shows pretraining largely determines how much RL compute actually helps.

They basically built a controlled LLM-style chess pipeline with pretraining, SFT reasoning traces, and GRPO on verifiable puzzles.

They also found that more pretraining tokens make RL improve faster. On top of that, RL sharpens easy cases, but can discover buried correct moves on hard ones.

read more:
https://t.co/Zt8JYiIRGO

x.name: Andrej Karpathy
x.bio: I like training large deep neural nets.

tweets.text: One pattern I find useful for working with LLMs is a nice long ramble session. Sometimes the LLM needs more bits to understand what you're trying to achieve, but you're too lazy to type them. In these cases I like to lean back, switch to /voice and just ramble for like 10 minutes, total mess, anything goes, full stream of consciousness. Sometimes I declare it up top, something like "switching to speech recognition sorry for any typos...". Sometimes I turn it into a small interview of a few turns. But I find that the LLMs are somehow very good at reconstructing long incoherent rambles and often their echo of your own tangle of thoughts comes out quite a bit cleaner than what you started with. The result is that you improve the mind meld and have to correct things less from that point on.

we've repeatedly seen that this is a useful separation of concerns: 

1/ the brain is a managed harness, well-tuned for Claude, auto-upgraded w/ each model release. 

2/ and hands are dispatched on-demand w/ data residency & network isolation. 

video:

x.bio: I build things @AnthropicAI, Co-Maintainer https://t.co/g4potti8nq

tweets.text:
It all takes less time than you might think, too. I kept he introduction as tight as possible. It probably takes about 90 minutes to read.

In about a day, you can do pre- and post-training on a model that'll write coherent responses.

Have you built a language model? You should. It's so much fun to chat with something you made.

Anyone can do it, too. I made an app that teaches the fundamentals and gives you everything you need to build your own: https://t.co/nLjFfpczbV https://t.co/akIMnTzOEG
